Package coxprogramming.processing.sprites

Class Vector2D

Object
Vector2D
public class Vector2D extends Object
A 2D vector â€” a pair of numbers (x, y) that can represent either a position (a point on the screen) or a direction with speed (like "move 3 pixels right and 2 pixels down each frame").

Every method returns a new Vector2D rather than changing the original, so you can chain calculations without worrying about overwriting values you still need.

Example â€” move a point one step along a direction: 

   Vector2D position  = new Vector2D(100, 200);
   Vector2D velocity  = new Vector2D(3, -1);
   Vector2D nextFrame = position.add(velocity);

    Returns the angle in radians you would need to rotate this vector to point in the same direction as other.

    Returns the angle this vector makes with the positive x-axis, in radians.

    Methods inherited from class Object

  • Field Details

    • x

      public float x
      The horizontal component. Positive x points to the right.

    • y

      public float y
      The vertical component. Positive y points downward (Processing convention).

  • Constructor Details

    • Vector2D

      public Vector2D(float x, float y)
      Creates a new Vector2D with the given x and y values.
      Parameters:
      x - the horizontal component
      y - the vertical component

  • Method Details

    • FromXY

      public static Vector2D FromXY(float x, float y)
      Creates a Vector2D from rectangular (Cartesian) coordinates. This is an alternative to calling new Vector2D(x, y) directly, and makes the intent explicit when working alongside FromPolar(float, float).

      Example: 

         Vector2D vel = Vector2D.FromXY(3, -2); // 3 right, 2 up

      Parameters:
      x - the horizontal component
      y - the vertical component
      Returns:
      a new Vector2D with the given x and y

    • FromPolar

      public static Vector2D FromPolar(float r, float theta)
      Creates a Vector2D from polar coordinates â€” a distance and an angle. Polar coordinates describe a vector by saying "go this far in this direction" rather than "go this far right and this far down".

      The angle is measured in radians from the positive x-axis (pointing right), counter-clockwise. Because Processing's y-axis points downward, an angle of 0 points right, and an angle of PI/2 points down.

      Example â€” a velocity of speed 5 pointing at 45 degrees: 

         Vector2D vel = Vector2D.FromPolar(5, PI / 4);

      Parameters:
      r - the magnitude (length) of the vector â€” how far from the origin
      theta - the angle in radians
      Returns:
      a new Vector2D with the equivalent x and y components

    • add

      public Vector2D add(Vector2D other)
      Adds this vector to other and returns the result. Think of it as combining two movements: if you walk 3 steps east then 2 steps east, you end up 5 steps east.
      Parameters:
      other - the vector to add
      Returns:
      a new Vector2D equal to (this.x + other.x, this.y + other.y)

    • subtract

      public Vector2D subtract(Vector2D other)
      Subtracts other from this vector and returns the result. This is useful for finding the direction from one point to another: target.subtract(myPosition) points from you toward the target.
      Parameters:
      other - the vector to subtract
      Returns:
      a new Vector2D equal to (this.x - other.x, this.y - other.y)

    • mag

      public float mag()
      Returns the magnitude (length) of this vector. Calculated using the Pythagorean theorem: sqrt(xÂ² + yÂ²). If this vector represents a velocity, mag() is the speed.
      Returns:
      the length of this vector as a non-negative float

    • dotProduct

      public float dotProduct(Vector2D other)
      Computes the dot product of this vector and other. The dot product tells you how much two vectors point in the same direction. A result of 0 means they are perpendicular; a large positive number means they point in very similar directions.
      Parameters:
      other - the other vector
      Returns:
      the dot product as a float

    • inverse

      public Vector2D inverse()
      Returns the additive inverse of this vector â€” the same length but pointing in the exact opposite direction. For example, the inverse of (3, -2) is (-3, 2).
      Returns:
      a new Vector2D pointing in the opposite direction

    • scale

      public Vector2D scale(float m)
      Multiplies both components by m, making the vector longer or shorter without changing its direction. Use a value between 0 and 1 to shrink it, or greater than 1 to grow it. A negative value also flips the direction.
      Parameters:
      m - the scaling factor
      Returns:
      a new Vector2D scaled by m

    • unitVector

      public Vector2D unitVector()
      Returns a unit vector â€” a vector pointing in the same direction as this one but with a length of exactly 1. This is handy when you want to move at a fixed speed toward a target: get the direction vector, call unitVector(), then scale(speed).

      Note: calling this on a zero-length vector (0, 0) will cause a division-by-zero error.

      Returns:
      a new unit Vector2D in the same direction

    • perpendicular

      public Vector2D perpendicular()
      Returns a vector that is perpendicular (at a 90-degree right angle) to this one, rotated clockwise.
      Returns:
      a new Vector2D perpendicular to this one

    • theta

      protected float theta()
      Returns the angle this vector makes with the positive x-axis, in radians. This is a helper used internally by angleTo(coxprogramming.processing.sprites.Vector2D).
      Returns:
      the angle in radians

    • angleTo

      public float angleTo(Vector2D other)
      Returns the angle in radians you would need to rotate this vector to point in the same direction as other. A positive result means rotate counter-clockwise.
      Parameters:
      other - the target vector
      Returns:
      the angle between the two vectors in radians

    • rotate

      public Vector2D rotate(float rads)
      Rotates this vector by rads radians around the origin and returns the result. Positive values rotate counter-clockwise. Uses the standard 2D rotation formula.
      Parameters:
      rads - the angle to rotate by, in radians
      Returns:
      a new rotated Vector2D
